text: At the risk of going against the flow, I want to put forth the suggestion that maybe Alan Kay isn't quite the messiah he's sometimes worked up to be. He's an intelligent man for sure, but to some degree eccentric, and it's unclear whether his advice is actually good advice. He did contribute to some major advances in the 20th century, but his achievements in recent decades are more minor, so it doesn't appear his formula is working out even for himself anymore.

One requirement of being a leader (including a thought leader) is being a good speaker and salesman. Alan Kay definitely fits that bill, but you have to peel back the presentation layer and look beneath to see if the underlying content is any good. Donald Trump is also a good salesman, but because we (tech people) are not his target audience, most of us here have likely concluded that the content he's selling is of questionable quality. With salesman who are targeting us (tech people) directly, I think it's harder to see the reality of the content. Personally, after casually buying Alan Kay's content for a few years, I've since become skeptical of it.

Okay i see better your approach. Very hard to comment without an example. I get triggered by people who marvel at Emacs or Lisp, both ancient technologies that follow no ergonomic principles, and were products of a priesthood mentality which kept programming a tiny field. I can remember the academics refusing to publish algorithms in a language in actual common use (they all published in Algol) so nobody outside the priesthood could learn, and also they used languages like LISP and arcane editors like Emacs which forced you to use lisp to do find/replace. We now share running code, and with YouTube tutorials we have 20 million active programmers instead of 20 thousand
that sounds very sinister
I don't think most people who advocate for Lisp are doing so because they want to exclude people. you can actually just like a design, even that of Emacs/Lisp
(fwiw, I also don't find Lisp/Emacs to be very well designed, but I don't think of people who do as sinister elitists)

<@UCGAK10LS>, I want to agree that Alan Kay, like anyone else, deserves both respect and critique.  For example, it's quite fair to consider whether STEPS was a good idea for a project.  What came up it?  What went right?  What went wrong?  Whether it was successful?  By what measure?  And what can we learn from it?

i looked over the source code for Bosque. Hardly any comments. Code is for computers, and comments are for people (including the author who months down the road may not remember why something was done). Maybe in big companies you can slip through the cracks. I was also surprised that Boeing bungled the software so bad for the MCAS system, a critical system that only read one of 2 sensors, and had numerous fatal - as in killing people - mistakes in their software. I sure hope that all the designers here make an effort in your new language to encourage people to comment their code.

<@UEG9ECNSU> Thanks. I haven’t seen the Sun Ra movie, but I’ll add it to my queue. About your gist: I feel similarly, wondering how my life would have been different if I lived somewhere more important, or conducted my time differently when I was younger. But <@UCGAK10LS> has the right take: don’t celebrate celebrity. If everyone in the world needs to end up in the top 1% of some field, then most people will live unfulfilled lives. Better to pick something you want to do well, and do it well on your own terms. I’ll be happy if the programming system I’m making helps my coworkers enjoy their job more. I don’t need to make an impact that lasts for 10,000 years — that’s an awful goal to choose for oneself.

I've worked on large code bases were comments were explicitly banned, or at least discouraged.  There are other ways to communicate the intent of code that are better than comments though.  This code base used a lot of runtime asserts to validate assumptions, and they often had messages, so they were comments of sorts.  Integration and unit tests with good coverage are better than comments.  I know that for a lot of people the answer is "do everything!" but there is a cost to that as well.

Edward, I understand what you mean! Bad usability / presentation is what is driving many of us to try to make things better. What I've described above is not the end goal, it's just the required mechanism to create a tool that can customize itself to *become* whatever is the best tool or representation for a whatever you want to use it for.

The idea is not for something Lispy to be what's actually presented. That's just a good structure for storing and data representation. I actually think if it more like JSON.

The idea is to be able to say "for this given code or data, draw this (perhaps very different) UI".

I respect his interdisciplinary approach and broad vision. I think insights come from unexpected places, in my case I've had a lot of programming insight from works of philosophy such as Quine and Hegel. I can't help but feel a whiff of "hipsterism" in his suggestions though ... they're so obscure, he points a computer scientist not just at Adam Smith but at lesser-known Adam Smith. The more obvious reading suggestions like say Knuth are absent, does he think it's not worth fitting in Knuth to your 200 a year because McLuhan is more relevant to CS, or is he just assuming that suggesting Knuth to a CS person is redundant?

I agree completely about making the kernel of the Web as simple as possible, I'm kind of baffled why there's so little progress in that direction, e.g. I haven't even heard of an academic project to build a small version of the web. NaCl was the closest thing to such an effort that I've heard of (I guess wasm takes up the mantle but most wasm people disclaim the idea of ever deprecating the overgrown web stack).

<@UH84GQ3CZ> - I got part way through responding about my project(s) before the conversation went elsewhere. I've now edited my original comment (above) with my full reply.

I have mixed feelings about comments. For one thing, inline comments are certainly limited ... I'd rather have literate programming with full support for graphics, typesetting, sectioning and so on. But, I also think that there's some wishful thinking when it comes to code documentation. "If only this were documented better, people could understand it with little effort."

The reason I think this is wishful thinking is basically the counterexample of TeX - a program that is meticulously documented in a printed book with extensive tests, examples, a companion book giving a high-level overview. All this effort seems to have failed to make it comprehensible. TeX code is rarely adapted to other purposes, people even want to rewrite TeX or extract the core algorithms into other languages.

The lesson I take from it is that a large, dense code base can not be understood at all without extreme effort. Unless it is someone's job or serious project to understand it, they will not do so no matter how well commented it is etc. If it is someone's job to understand it then they will be glad for the comments but it will still be a huge effort.

So I think comments/documentation are important, but far less important than simpler, smaller code bases.

Tex is one of the worst systems ever devised. The Eve project adopted markdown as the commenting system, and i think that is a reasonable way to offer graphical commenting without a lot of baggage. The idea of banning comments in a large code base is preposterous. I used to make a living fixing up other people's problems as a consultant, and after doing that work for many years, you learn what is really important. Single letter parameter names, with no comments, are absolutely deadly to comprehension, and i consider them to be intentional obfuscation.

Also Smalltalk-OO is an attempt to finding a perspective very different from 'data structures + procedures'.
> I thought of the whole as the entire computer and wondered why anyone would want to divide it up into weaker things called data structures and procedures.
So, to put it in meme form: Knuth is tired.

<@UH84GQ3CZ> - Perhaps. I think there is a leap here: "if you are trying to write a new programming tool or language or system, then I think you'll find yourself working with syntax trees and grammars". Yes if you go down this path certainly Knuth etc. are useful. But how do you choose which path you want to explore or what different paths are even available? Are there other paths where you have programmable systems *without* complex parsing? What could these look like? For the broader problem I think we could take a holistic outlook where computation machines, cognitive psychology, HCI all blur together.

One major difference between my two projects is that the second (Interact) keeps a history: every edit or manipulation is saved as an "action". Any change causes everything to be recomputed by reapplying all actions in order. Code is made of actions, so there is zero difference between user interaction and "programming", and it's like the program is in constant debug-mode as you edit it.

My personal opinion is that if lots of comments are needed, then the code must be hard to understand, which means they're is something wrong with the code (e.g. poorly factored). Adding comments will not fix that, and just adds noise. If the code IS understandable, then the comments are not needed either.

There definitely are cases where the occasional brief comment is helpful (not all intents are clear in all cases). And there are some cases where larger comments are needed. But that is not most cases.

It is definitely important to communicate intent though, and the method for doing so depends on the context. But personally, I think that code and source control history (commit messages) should be the primary sources.

Some code is just complex and should be commented, sometimes with links to references.  I think it makes sense for math heavy code that might need diagrams or dense optimized code (especially assembly language or SIMD intrinsics) that needs explanation and perhaps a higher level side by side implementation.  I'm also distinguishing notes (one or two lines that say why you did something) versus... what I think some of you are advocating... a more literate coding style or embedded documentation
